> *Your current cash covers about 5.6 months of observed spending—very close to a six-month reserve.*

I’m Arthur. Based on your last 12 complete months, you spend **$5,400 per month**. That makes your emergency-savings range:

| Coverage | Target |
|---|---:|
| 3 months | $16,200 |
| 6 months | $32,400 |
| Current checking + savings | $30,500 |
| Gap to 6 months | **$1,900** |

For a household with a spouse and two young children, I recommend the **six-month target of $32,400**, rather than stopping at three months. You’re already **94% there**. At your observed **$900 monthly surplus**, you can close the remaining gap in just over **two months**.

Keep this reserve in an insured, readily accessible savings account—not invested—and avoid counting cash needed for upcoming bills. Once you reach **$32,400**, stop adding to the emergency fund unless your essential monthly costs rise.

This estimate uses all observed spending, which is conservative. Only one banking institution is visible, so income or expenses routed through another bank may change the target.

This is educational financial guidance; I’m not a licensed financial advisor.
